1:0.rendering: I said in my heart, "Come now, I will test you with pleasure, so enjoy yourself." But look, that too was vapour.
1:0.note: The Hebrew word for "pleasure" here (simkhah) echoes the joy that fills the creation story in Genesis, but the Preacher runs the experiment and finds it empty, like vapour that vanishes. This sets up the book's relentless quest to find meaning under the sun, a thread that will ultimately point toward the gift of joy that comes from outside ourselves, a theme Jesus later embodies as the one who offers life to the full.

1:1.rendering: I said of laughter, "It's madness," and of pleasure, "What does it accomplish?"
1:1.note: In the ancient Near Eastern world where kings threw lavish parties to display their power, the Preacher experiments with "gladness" and "pleasure" (simcha and simchah in Hebrew, the same root that will later describe the joy of God's people in the restored creation). He finds they lead nowhere on their own, exposing the futility of chasing joy apart from the Giver, a thread that runs through the wisdom literature and finds its surprising resolution in the one who turns water into wine and invites us into lasting joy.

1:2.rendering: I explored in my heart by indulging my body with wine, while my mind still guided me with wisdom, and by taking hold of folly, until I could see what was good for the children of humanity to do under the heavens during the few days of their lives.
1:2.note: The Hebrew word for "explored" here is the same one used in Genesis 2 for the man "working" the garden, but now Qohelet is working the experiment of pleasure and wisdom side by side, like a scientist testing soil. This verse sits at the heart of the book's quest to find lasting good under the sun, a search that will eventually reveal humanity's need for something beyond our own striving, pointing forward to the rest that Jesus offers those exhausted by life "under the heavens."

1:3.rendering: I made my works great. I built houses for myself. I planted vineyards for myself.
1:3.note: The Hebrew word for "made" here is the same one used in Genesis 1 when God "made" the heavens and earth, creating an ironic echo: the king who tries to play creator finds that his grand building projects, like everything under the sun, ultimately lead to the same vapour-like end.

1:4.rendering: I made gardens and parks for myself, and I planted in them every kind of fruit tree.
1:4.note: The Hebrew word for "parks" here is pardes, an ancient Persian loanword that also shows up in the story of the garden in Song of Songs and the restored garden of God in Revelation. This moment in Ecclesiastes is part of the teacher's grand experiment to create his own personal Eden, a self-made paradise where heaven and earth overlap through human wisdom and effort alone, only to discover that it too leads to the same vapour-like frustration that began in Genesis 3.

1:5.rendering: I made pools of water for myself, to water from them a forest sprouting with trees.
1:5.note: The Hebrew word for "pools" (berikhot) echoes the gathering of waters in Genesis 1, where God separates seas so the land can sprout with vegetation, yet here the Teacher is the one engineering his own paradise garden, complete with a "forest sprouting with trees." This little verse quietly participates in the Bible's larger pattern of humans reaching for godlike control over creation, a theme that runs from Eden to the new creation where the river of life flows freely from God's throne, not ours.

1:6.rendering: I bought male and female slaves, and I had slaves born in my house. I also had abundant herds of cattle and flocks of sheep, more than all who were in Jerusalem before me.
1:6.note: The Hebrew word for "slaves" (avadim) is the same root used for "servants" throughout the Old Testament, echoing the Israelites' own slavery in Egypt and their identity as "servants" of God. In the unified biblical story this moment in Ecclesiastes shows humanity's quest for meaning through dominating others and accumulating image-of-God bearers as property, a pattern that finds its surprising resolution in Jesus, who came not to be served but to serve and to set captives free.

1:7.rendering: I gathered silver and gold for myself, the treasure of kings and provinces. I got myself male and female singers, and the delights of the sons of man: many concubines.
1:7.note: The Hebrew word for "delights" here (ta'anug) is rare and points to sensual luxury, echoing the garden-of-Eden abundance that humans keep trying to seize for themselves. This verse sits right in the middle of Qoheleth's grand experiment with wisdom, pleasure, and work, showing how the quest for more always collides with the limits of our mortality.

1:8.rendering: So I became greater and more powerful than anyone who had been in Jerusalem before me. Yet my wisdom remained with me.
1:8.note: The word "greater" here (Hebrew gadal) echoes the language of Genesis 26:13 where Isaac "grew" (gadal) and became very great, and it foreshadows the rise of Solomon's kingdom in 1 Kings, but Qohelet quickly discovers that increased status and wisdom do not escape the "hevel" (vapour-like futility) that touches every human pursuit under the sun.

1:9.rendering: And whatever my eyes desired, I did not withhold from them. I did not restrain my heart from any pleasure, for my heart found pleasure in all my toil, and this was my reward from all my toil.
1:9.note: The word for "pleasure" here is the same one used for "delight" when God looks at his good creation in Genesis 1, yet the Teacher's experiment in grabbing every delight under the sun ends in the same "chasing after wind" that marks so much of life east of Eden. This ancient royal quest echoes through the biblical story, revealing how even the best of human wisdom and wealth cannot restore the heaven-and-earth harmony we were made for, a longing that finds its surprising answer in Jesus.

1:10.rendering: I turned to look at all the things my hands had made, at all the hard work I had done, and look, it was all vapour, a chasing after wind. There was no lasting profit under the sun.
1:10.note: The Hebrew word hevel, often translated "vanity" or "meaningless," literally means "vapour" or "breath," echoing the name Abel from Genesis 4 whose life was cut short like mist. Qoheleth's experiment with wisdom, pleasure, and achievement shows how every human pursuit apart from God slips through our fingers, pointing us forward to the one who would later say he came to give life that is truly life.

1:11.rendering: Then I turned to see wisdom and madness and folly, for what can the man do who comes after the king? Only what has already been done.
1:11.note: The word for "turned" here (panah in Hebrew) signals a deliberate shift in Qoheleth's investigation, like a researcher changing his angle on the same problem. This verse sits at the pivot between his failed quest for lasting meaning through pleasure and wealth (Ecclesiastes 2:1-11) and his examination of wisdom itself, echoing the garden-of-Eden pattern of humans grasping for knowledge that only God can give, a thread that runs from Genesis through the wisdom literature and finds its surprising resolution in the one the Gospels call "greater than Solomon."

1:12.rendering: Then I saw that wisdom is better than folly, just as light is better than darkness.
1:12.note: The Hebrew word for "saw" here (ra'ah) is the same one used when God "saw" his creation was good in Genesis, inviting us to notice Qohelet's experiment as a kind of anti-creation project. This verse launches a wisdom-versus-folly thread that echoes through the book, showing that even the best human insight cannot escape the "vanity" of life under the sun, a tension the New Testament resolves when Jesus claims to be the light of the world who overcomes the darkness we cannot.

1:13.rendering: The wise person has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. Yet I also realized that one fate happens to them all.
1:13.note: The Hebrew word for "eyes" here echoes the image-of-God theme from Genesis, where humans were meant to see and rule wisely over creation. Qohelet watches this wisdom-darkness contrast play out, only to discover that death levels both the insightful and the oblivious, a tension the biblical story holds until Jesus steps into our darkness as the light that no grave can overcome.

1:14.rendering: Then I saw that the fate of the fool and my own fate are the same, so why have I been so exceedingly wise? I said to myself, this also is vapour.
1:14.note: The Hebrew word for vapour, hevel, echoes the name of Abel in Genesis 4, the innocent shepherd whose life was cut short, reminding us that even wisdom cannot escape the fleeting, breath-like nature of life under the sun.

1:15.rendering: For there is no lasting remembrance of the wise or the fool in the days to come. In the days that are already coming, all will be forgotten. So how can the wise die just like the fool?
1:15.note: The Preacher here picks up the Hebrew word for "remembrance" (zeker) that echoes through Israel's story, from God's covenantal memory of Noah to the command to "remember" the exodus, yet he stares at the grave and sees even the wise man's legacy dissolve into the same oblivion as the fool's.

1:16.rendering: So I hated life, because the work that is done under the sun was grievous to me. All of it is vapour, a chasing after wind.
1:16.note: The word translated "hated" here carries the force of deep revulsion, the same Hebrew root used when Esau's wives become a source of grief to Isaac and Rebekah. Qoheleth's despair echoes the frustration of Genesis 3, where humanity's work is now "toilsome" and the ground yields thorns. This raw confession of life's vapour-like nature sets the stage for the book's wisdom journey, ultimately pointing toward the one who would enter the frustration of our world to redeem it from futility.

1:17.rendering: I hated all my toil that I toiled under the sun, for I must leave it to the person who comes after me.
1:17.note: The word for "hated" here is the same strong term used for hating evil or an enemy, revealing the Preacher's visceral disgust at life's futility. This despair echoes the curse of Genesis 3 where human toil yields only thorns and sweat, yet it sets the stage for the larger biblical story where the ultimate heir, Jesus, receives the inheritance of a new creation that no one can steal or ruin.

1:18.rendering: And who knows whether he will be a wise person or a fool? Yet he will rule over all the fruit of my toil and my wisdom that I toiled for under the sun. This also is vapour.
1:18.note: The word "who knows" echoes the resigned question in Ecclesiastes 3:21 and 6:12, highlighting the frustrating limits of human foresight. This verse deepens the book's meditation on legacy and futility: the Preacher's hard-won wisdom and wealth will pass to an unknown heir, a theme that runs from Genesis's cursed toil to the New Testament's hope that our labor in the Lord is not ultimately vapour.

1:19.rendering: So I turned my heart to despair over all the hard work that I had done under the sun.
1:19.note: The word for "despair" here (ya'ash) carries the sense of letting go of hope, a dark pivot in the Teacher's quest. This moment echoes the Genesis 3 curse of painful toil and foreshadows the larger biblical story where human striving apart from God leads to futility, until Jesus enters the cursed labor of the world and offers rest to those weary from their endless "under the sun" pursuits.

1:20.rendering: For there is a person whose toil is with wisdom, knowledge, and skill, yet he must leave his portion to someone who did not toil for it. This too is vapour, and a great evil.
1:20.note: The Hebrew word for "toil" (amal) echoes the curse after Eden (Genesis 3:17), portraying human labor under the sun as both exhausting and ultimately futile. This verse sits at the heart of Qoheleth's quest, exposing the fracture in our image-of-God calling to rule and create: we pour ourselves into work that should endure, only for death to hand the results to another, a pattern the Bible will later resolve when Jesus, the true wise king, toils on the cross and leaves an inheritance that no one can steal.

1:21.rendering: For what does a person get from all the toil and from the striving of his heart with which he toils under the sun?
1:21.note: The Hebrew word for "toil" here, amal, carries the heavy sense of exhausting, frustrating labor, echoing back to the cursed ground and sweat of Adam's face in Genesis 3. This question sits at the heart of the Teacher's quest to find lasting meaning in human work, exposing the vapour-like nature of life under the sun and pointing forward to the New Testament hope that our labor is not in vain in the Lord (1 Corinthians 15:58).

1:22.rendering: For all his days are full of pain, and his work is vexation. Even at night his heart does not rest. This too is vapour.
1:22.note: The Hebrew word for vapour (hevel) echoes the book's opening poem and the name of Adam's son Abel, whose short life ended in violence. Qohelet is tracing how the curse of Genesis 3, thorns and toil and restless hearts, still hangs over human work under the sun, yet this very frustration is what drives the unified biblical story forward toward the one who would enter our toil and give rest to our hearts.

1:23.rendering: There is nothing better for a person than to eat and drink and find satisfaction in all their toil. This too, I saw, is from the hand of God.
1:23.note: The Hebrew word for "satisfaction" here is the same one used for the "profit" or "advantage" Qohelet has been hunting for throughout the book, creating a subtle wordplay that reframes enjoyment not as escape but as a gift handed down from the Creator. This moment of rest amid the cycles of toil echoes the seventh-day rest of Genesis 2 and points forward to Jesus' invitation to find true rest in him, where heaven-and-earth overlap in the ordinary rhythms of human life.

1:24.rendering: For who can eat or who can enjoy themselves apart from him?
1:24.note: The Hebrew word here for "enjoy" (or "experience pleasure") is the same root that names the Garden of Eden, hinting that true delight has always been a gift tied to the Creator's presence. This verse sits at the heart of the Teacher's quest, exposing how even the best gifts of food, work, and wisdom become empty when unhooked from the Giver, a thread that runs from Eden's loss all the way to Jesus, who invites us back to abundant life in relationship with the Father.

1:25.rendering: For to the person who is good before his face, he gives wisdom and knowledge and joy. But to the sinner he gives the task of gathering and collecting only to give it to the one who is good before God. This too is vapour, a chasing after wind.
1:25.note: The key Hebrew word here for "good before his face" plays on the idea of being pleasing in God's sight, echoing how the story of Solomon began in 1 Kings 3. This verse exposes the frustrating pattern of human life under the sun, where even wisdom, knowledge, and joy are gifts from the Creator, and the sinner's toil ultimately benefits the faithful, pointing forward to the larger biblical theme that God is the one who truly gives inheritance and rest.

0:0.rendering: The words of the gatherer, son of David, king in Jerusalem.
0:0.note: The Hebrew title Qohelet, translated as "the gatherer" or "assembler," carries the sense of one who calls people together to collect and ponder life's observations, much like a shepherd gathering a flock. This mysterious royal figure, presented as a son of David reigning in Jerusalem, immediately ties the book into the wisdom tradition of Solomon while inviting us to overhear a lifelong meditation on the patterns of creation, human striving, and the fear of the Lord that echoes throughout the Hebrew Scriptures and finds its ultimate gathering point in Jesus.

0:1.rendering: Absolute vapour, says the Teacher, absolute vapour. Everything is vapour.
0:1.note: The Hebrew word hevel (often translated "vanity" or "meaningless") literally means "vapour" or "breath" - like the mist you see on a cold morning that vanishes in an instant. This opening line sets the stage for the whole book, inviting us to wrestle with life's fleeting nature under the sun, a theme that echoes the curse of Genesis 3 where humanity's return to dust highlights our need for the one who would later breathe new life into creation.

0:2.rendering: What does a person gain from all their toil that they toil under the sun?
0:2.note: The Hebrew word for "gain" or "profit" (yitron) echoes the language of merchants and traders, asking what lasting surplus remains from our endless labor under the sun. This question launches Ecclesiastes' exploration of life's vapour-like nature, inviting us to wonder what could truly satisfy if everything cycles back to dust, a longing the unified biblical story ultimately meets in the one who offers rest beyond our toil.

0:3.rendering: A generation goes and a generation comes, but the earth remains forever.
0:3.note: The Hebrew word for "generation" (dor) carries the idea of a circling or turning, creating a subtle wordplay with the book's theme of endless cycles. This verse opens the book's exploration of life under the sun, where human life feels fleeting and repetitive against the backdrop of an enduring earth, setting the stage for the unified biblical story that ultimately finds its resolution in Jesus, who breaks the cycles of futility through his resurrection.

0:4.rendering: The sun rises and the sun goes down, and it hurries back to the place where it rises again.
0:4.note: This verse captures the Hebrew word for "hurries" or "pants," evoking a breathless chase that echoes the restless motion of creation in Genesis 1, yet here it highlights the repetitive cycles of the sun that never quite break free into something new. In the unified story of Scripture, this meditation on endless return sets the stage for the human longing that finds its ultimate rest in Jesus, who brings the new creation where the old patterns of futility are finally transformed.

0:5.rendering: The wind goes to the south, and circles around to the north. It circles and circles on its rounds, and the wind returns on its circuits.
0:5.note: The Hebrew word for "wind" here is ruach, the same word used for God's Spirit hovering over the waters in Genesis 1 and breathing life into humanity. Qohelet watches this ceaseless, circular motion of ruach and sees in it the repetitive patterns of our world under the sun, a meditation that echoes the creation story while highlighting our human struggle to find meaning within those patterns, a longing the New Testament will later portray as creation itself groaning for liberation in Jesus.

0:6.rendering: All the streams flow to the sea, yet the sea is never full. To the place where the streams flow, there they flow again.
0:6.note: The Hebrew word for "streams" (nachal) echoes the river that flowed out of Eden to water the garden in Genesis 2, yet here the ceaseless, repetitive motion of water leads only to futility under the sun. This image of endless return without fulfillment sets up the book's quest to find meaning in a broken creation, a longing that finds its deeper answer in Jesus, who offers living water that finally satisfies.

0:7.rendering: All things are wearisome. A person cannot speak of it. The eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.
0:7.note: The Hebrew word for "wearisome" here is the same root as the book's famous title "hevel" (vapour or breath), evoking how the endless cycles of life leave us chasing after wind. This ancient meditation on human limits echoes the garden story's insight that life east of Eden feels incomplete, setting the stage for the unified biblical hope that only the Creator can truly satisfy our restless eyes and ears, a longing Jesus later addresses as the bread and water of life.

0:8.rendering: What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done, and there is nothing new under the sun.
0:8.note: The Teacher's refrain that there is "nothing new under the sun" deliberately echoes the Genesis creation language of "the heavens and the earth," inviting us to see the endless cycles of toil and repetition as the frustrating reality of life in a broken creation, a wisdom observation that sets the stage for the Bible's larger story of how the Creator will one day make all things new.

0:9.rendering: Is there a thing of which one can say, "See this, it is new"? It has already been, in the ages before us.
0:9.note: The Preacher's rhetorical question plays on the Hebrew word for "already" (kevar), underscoring the relentless cycles of the sun, wind, and rivers in verses 4-9, a wisdom observation that echoes Genesis 1's ordered creation while hinting at humanity's restless search for meaning under the sun; this ancient meditation on novelty's illusion sets the stage for the book's honest wrestle with life's vapour-like quality, ultimately finding its deeper resolution in Jesus who makes all things new.

0:10.rendering: There is no remembrance of the people who came before us, and even those who come after will not be remembered by those who come after them.
0:10.note: The word for "remembrance" (zikron) echoes the key biblical theme of covenant memory, yet here the Teacher shows us its frustrating limits under the sun. This verse sits at the start of Ecclesiastes' exploration of hevel, inviting us into the wisdom journey of holding life's apparent meaninglessness in tension with the larger biblical story that moves toward a future where God will remember his people and wipe away every tear.

0:11.rendering: I, the Teacher, have been king over Israel in Jerusalem.
0:11.note: The Hebrew word for "Teacher" (Qohelet) comes from the verb "to assemble" or "to gather a congregation," portraying this kingly figure as one who gathers people to ponder life's riddles. This sets up the book's exploration of human wisdom under the sun, echoing the royal experiments of Solomon while inviting us into a meditation that finds its deeper resolution in the one greater than Solomon who gathers all things in himself.

0:12.rendering: I set my heart to seek and to explore by wisdom all that is done under the heavens. It is an evil task that God has given to the sons of humanity to be occupied with.
0:12.note: The Hebrew word for "set my heart" (natati et libbi) echoes the language of wholehearted devotion found in Deuteronomy, but here Qohelet turns that intense focus toward unraveling the patterns of life "under the heavens." This sets up the book's exploration of the human struggle to make sense of a fractured world, a wisdom quest that finds its ultimate answer in the one who later says he has overcome the world.

0:13.rendering: I saw all the deeds that are done under the sun, and look, everything is vapour, a chasing after wind.
0:13.note: The Hebrew word hevel (vapour or breath) echoes back to the fleeting mist of Genesis 4's Abel, whose name is the same word, and forward to the fragile life that Jesus took on in the incarnation, showing how the Teacher's honest wrestling with futility is part of the unified story that finds its resolution when heaven and earth finally overlap in the new creation.

0:14.rendering: What is crooked cannot be straightened, and what is lacking cannot be counted.
0:14.note: The Teacher opens his quest by observing the relentless cycles of creation, and this proverb uses two Hebrew words that echo the Genesis creation story: "crooked" (or twisted) recalls how humanity bent what God called "good," while "lacking" or "deficient" hints at the incompleteness that entered the world. It sets up the Bible's larger pattern of exile and return, where human efforts to fix the bent world on our own prove futile, yet the story ultimately moves toward the One who can make all things new.

0:15.rendering: I spoke with my heart, saying, "Look, I have become great and increased wisdom more than all who were over Jerusalem before me, and my heart has seen much wisdom and knowledge."
0:15.note: The Hebrew phrase "dibberti ani im libbi" (I spoke with my heart) reveals Qohelet's inner dialogue as he tests the limits of human wisdom, echoing the royal experiments of kings like Solomon while exposing the ancient quest for knowledge that stretches all the way back to the tree in Eden. This sets up the book's unified exploration of life "under the sun," where even multiplied wisdom cannot escape the frustrating cycles of a broken world, a tension the New Testament will later resolve in the one who became wisdom from God.

0:16.rendering: So I gave my heart to know wisdom, and to know madness and folly. I came to see that even this is chasing after wind.
0:16.note: The Hebrew word for "heart" here is lev, the seat of the mind and will, showing the Teacher's total, relentless pursuit of understanding. This verse echoes the garden story in Genesis where humanity reached for the knowledge of good and evil, only to discover that such wisdom apart from God is like trying to catch the wind, a theme that runs through the Bible until Jesus embodies the true wisdom that brings order to the chaos.

0:17.rendering: Because in much wisdom is much vexation, and the one who increases knowledge increases pain.
0:17.note: The Hebrew word for "vexation" here is the same one used for "anger" or "frustration" elsewhere in the Bible, creating a wordplay that links growing insight with growing sorrow, much like Adam and Eve's eyes being opened in Genesis 3 only to discover their own nakedness and exile. This sets the stage for the unified biblical story's tension between wisdom as a gift from God and wisdom's painful awareness of the world's brokenness, a thread that finds its surprising resolution in Jesus, who grew in wisdom yet bore the ultimate pain of that knowledge on the cross.

2:0.rendering: There is a time for everything, and a season for every matter under heaven.
2:0.note: The Hebrew word for "time" here is et, which carries the sense of an appointed or fitting moment rather than just the ticking clock, echoing the ordered rhythm of creation in Genesis 1 where God separates and appoints times and purposes. This poem in Ecclesiastes invites us to embrace the beautiful, sometimes painful, seasons of human life as part of the unified story where the Creator weaves all things toward their ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, who entered our time to redeem it.

2:1.rendering: A time to give birth, and a time to die. A time to plant, and a time to uproot what is planted.
2:1.note: The Hebrew word 'et' (time) repeats like a steady drumbeat, framing all of human life within God's appointed rhythms, much like the ordered cycles of creation in Genesis 1. This poem echoes the image-of-God mandate to 'be fruitful and multiply' and to cultivate the ground, yet holds both life and death, planting and uprooting, in tension, pointing forward to the one who would enter our time-bound story to bring new creation out of death.

2:2.rendering: A time to tear down and a time to build up, a time to kill and a time to heal.
2:2.note: The Hebrew word for "time" here, et, carries a sense of the right or appointed moment, echoing the rhythms of creation and covenant in Genesis where God both tears down chaos to build order and brings life out of death. This verse sits at the heart of Ecclesiastes' meditation on the seasons of human existence under the sun, pointing forward to Jesus who embodies both tearing down the old order in his death and building up new creation in his resurrection.

2:3.rendering: A time to weep, and a time to laugh, a time to mourn, and a time to dance.
2:3.note: This verse sits in the poem's rhythmic catalog of 'times' that together portray the full range of human experience under the sun, echoing the creation pattern of order amid apparent chaos, much like the alternating seasons of joy and sorrow that mark Israel's story from exodus joy to exile lament and back again.

2:4.rendering: a time to throw stones away, and a time to gather stones together; a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing.
2:4.note: The Hebrew word for "stones" (evenim) echoes the story of Israel's ancestors building altars and memorials with stones to mark moments when heaven and earth overlapped. This verse sits in the poem's rhythm of opposites, inviting us to notice the wisdom of discerning the right moment in the human story, a wisdom that finds its ultimate expression in Jesus who knew when to gather his friends close and when to release them into the world.

2:5.rendering: A time to search and a time to give up searching, a time to keep and a time to throw away.
2:5.note: The Hebrew word for "search" here (baqash) echoes throughout the book as humanity's restless quest for meaning under the sun, while "give up" carries the sense of letting go or losing something precious. This pairs with the next line's keep-and-throw-away rhythm, inviting us into the wisdom of knowing when to hold tightly to God's gifts and when to release them, a tension that finds its ultimate resolution in Jesus who both seeks the lost and teaches us to lose our lives for his sake.

2:6.rendering: A time to tear and a time to mend, a time to be silent and a time to speak.
2:6.note: The Hebrew word for “tear” (qaraʿ) echoes the dramatic rending of garments in grief or repentance throughout the Old Testament, from Joshua and David to the prophets. In the unified story, this pairs with the later moment when the temple veil is torn from top to bottom at Jesus’ death, signaling the end of one era of separation and the beginning of new access between heaven and earth.

2:7.rendering: a time to love and a time to hate, a time for war and a time for peace.
2:7.note: The Hebrew word for "time" here (et) echoes throughout the poem, painting seasons as part of the ordered rhythm of God's good world, yet one that feels frustratingly out of our control. This pairs love with hate and war with peace in ways that startle us, reminding us that even the darkest human realities have their place under the sun, until the Prince of Peace arrives to finally reorder all things.

2:8.rendering: What advantage does the worker have in exchange for the work he does?
2:8.note: This question echoes the word "profit" or "advantage" (yitron in Hebrew) that runs like a refrain through Ecclesiastes, probing whether human toil under the sun adds up to anything lasting. It sits at the hinge of the famous poem on seasons, inviting us to wonder if our labor fits into a larger pattern only the Creator fully sees, a thread that later finds its answer in the New Testament vision of work offered to the Lord.

2:9.rendering: I have seen the task that God has given to the children of humanity to be busy with.
2:9.note: The word translated "task" here is the same Hebrew word for "business" or "occupation" that appears throughout Ecclesiastes, echoing the frustration of humanity's endless toil under the sun. This verse sits at the heart of the poem on seasons, showing how God has woven time and labor into the human experience, a pattern that finds its deeper resolution in Jesus who invites us to rest from our striving.

2:10.rendering: He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also put eternity into their hearts, yet so that no one can find out the work that God has done from beginning to end.
2:10.note: The Hebrew word 'olam' here, often translated 'eternity,' carries the sense of hidden, distant time that stretches beyond our grasp. This verse sits at the center of Ecclesiastes' poem about seasons, showing how God has wired humans with an awareness of the full sweep of time while keeping the bigger picture veiled, a theme that echoes through Israel's story of exile and return, and finds its resolution in Jesus who reveals the end from the beginning.

2:11.rendering: I know that there is nothing better for them than to be glad and to do good in their lives.
2:11.note: The key Hebrew word here for "good" (tov) echoes God's repeated declaration in Genesis 1 that creation is "good," inviting us to participate in that original goodness through simple joy and moral action even under the sun. This verse sits at the heart of Ecclesiastes' wisdom, showing how the Preacher finds fleeting but real meaning in the rhythms of time by embracing what God has given, a thread that later finds its full expression in Jesus' invitation to abundant life amid the brokenness of our world.

2:12.rendering: And also that everyone should eat and drink and find good in all their toil. This is a gift from God.
2:12.note: The Hebrew word for "gift" here is the same one used for the divine favor that rests on humanity in the creation story. Amid the poem's rhythmic cycles of time in Ecclesiastes 3, this verse echoes Genesis 1-2, where God gifts humanity the good work of stewarding creation and enjoying its fruit, a thread of divine generosity that runs through the whole biblical story and finds its fullest expression in Jesus.

2:13.rendering: I know that everything God does will last forever. Nothing can be added to it, and nothing can be taken away from it. God has done this so that people will stand in awe before him.
2:13.note: The word for "forever" here is olam, and the Preacher is playing with the idea that God's works stand outside the endless cycles of toil and death described in the rest of the book. This verse quietly echoes the creation account where God declares his work "very good" and sets boundaries that cannot be undone, pointing forward to the unified biblical theme that the Creator is faithfully stitching together a story that will not unravel, ultimately revealed in Jesus as the one through whom all things hold together.

2:14.rendering: Whatever has been, it is already; and whatever is going to be, it was already. And God seeks out what has been pursued.
2:14.note: The Hebrew word for "seeks out" (baqash) carries the sense of God carefully investigating or even "driving after" what has been driven away, a subtle echo of his pursuit of humanity after the exile-like scattering of Genesis 3. This verse sits at the center of Ecclesiastes' poem on times and seasons, revealing how the Teacher sees the relentless cycles of life not as meaningless repetition but as something God himself is actively weaving into his unified story that will one day find its resolution in Jesus, the one who steps into time to redeem what was lost.

2:15.rendering: I saw something else under the sun: in the place of justice there was wickedness, and in the place of righteousness there was wickedness.
2:15.note: The Teacher stares straight at the twisted places where human systems meant to uphold justice and righteousness have instead become engines of oppression, a pattern that echoes the fractured image-of-God rule we see from Genesis 3 onward. This observation sits at the heart of Ecclesiastes' wisdom, holding the tension between the good order God designed and the crooked reality we experience until the Messiah comes to set all things right.

2:16.rendering: I said in my heart, God will judge the righteous and the wicked, for there is a time for every matter and for every deed.
2:16.note: The Hebrew word for "time" here, et, echoes the poem's refrain from earlier in the chapter, creating a subtle wordplay that ties human activity to divine order. This verse holds the tension between the apparent randomness of life under the sun and the ancient hope of a coming judge, a thread that weaves through Israel's story and finds its surprising fulfillment when Jesus steps into history as the one appointed to judge the living and the dead.

2:17.rendering: I said in my heart concerning the sons of man, that God is testing them, and that they might see they are but animals.
2:17.note: The Hebrew word for "animals" here is behemah, the same term used in Genesis 1 for the livestock and wild beasts God made on day six, right before forming humans as his image. Qohelet stares at the shared fate of death that humans and animals have in common, and he wonders if this is God's way of exposing our illusion of superiority, a thread that runs from creation's goodness through the curse of Genesis 3 and finds its ultimate resolution when the Son of Man comes as the true image who shares our flesh yet defeats death for us all.

2:18.rendering: For the fate of the sons of man and the fate of the beast is one fate. As one dies, so dies the other. They all have one breath. Man has no advantage over the beast, for all is vapour.
2:18.note: The Hebrew word for "fate" (miqreh) here plays on the idea of "happening" or "meeting," echoing the Teacher's earlier observation that death is the great equalizer that levels every human project, much like how the flood in Genesis 6-9 showed all flesh sharing the same breath and returning to dust.

2:19.rendering: All go to one place. All are from the dust, and all return to dust.
2:19.note: The Teacher echoes Genesis 3:19 word for word, where humanity's rebellion leads to the ground (adamah) reclaiming the human (adam). This shared fate of dust-to-dust binds all creatures in the unified story of creation, curse, and mortality, a thread that finds its surprising resolution when the last Adam rises from the dust, conquering the grave.

2:20.rendering: Who knows whether the spirit of the sons of man goes upward and the spirit of the animal goes down into the earth?
2:20.note: The Hebrew word ruach, meaning both breath and spirit, creates a haunting wordplay here as Qohelet stares at the shared fate of humans and animals under the sun, their life-breath returning to dust. This question echoes Genesis 2-3 and the cursed ground of exile, pressing us to wonder if there is any lasting distinction between image-bearers and beasts, a tension the unified biblical story will resolve when Jesus breathes new resurrection life into humanity.

2:21.rendering: So I saw that there is nothing better for a person than to rejoice in their work, because that is their portion. For who can bring them to see what will be after them?
2:21.note: The Hebrew word for "portion" (cheleq) echoes the language of Israel's inheritance in the land, yet here the Preacher locates our true share right in the work and joy of the present moment under the sun. This verse sits at the climax of the poem on time in chapter 3, inviting us to embrace the gift of today while the larger biblical story quietly points forward to the day when the curse on toil is lifted and our labor participates in God's new creation.
